source_paper_title: Improving Envy Freeness up to Any Good Guarantees Through Rainbow Cycle Number
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Chaudhury_2024, title={Improving Envy Freeness up to Any Good Guarantees Through Rainbow Cycle Number}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2021.0252}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2021.0252}, number={4}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Chaudhury, Bhaskar Ray and Garg, Jugal and Mehlhorn, Kurt and Mehta, Ruta and Misra, Pranabendu}, year={2024}, month=nov, pages={2323–2340} }
context:
Page 5, end of Section 1.2 (immediately after the discussion of \(R(2)\le 2\)); reiterated on page 16 right after Theorem 6 in Section 5.

We suspect that \(R(d)\in O(d)\). We believe that finding better upper bounds on \(R(d)\) is a natural combinatorial question, and better upper bounds to \(R(d)\) imply the existence of better relaxations of EFX allocations. Therefore, investigating better upper bounds on the rainbow cycle number is of interest in its own right, and we leave this as an interesting problem.
title: Determine the asymptotically tight rainbow cycle number in bounded-partite digraphs
background_latex:
Fix an integer \(d\ge 1\). A directed \(k\)-partite graph is a digraph \(G=(V,E)\) whose vertex set is partitioned into \(k\) (nonempty) parts \(V_1,\dots,V_k\), and edges may go between any ordered pair of vertices (including across parts), with no restriction on directions.

Consider directed \(k\)-partite graphs \(G=(\bigcup_{i=1}^k V_i,E)\) satisfying the following properties:

1) (Bounded part size) \(|V_i|\le d\) for all \(i\in\{1,\dots,k\}\).

2) (Pairwise in-neighborhood condition) For any two distinct parts \(V_i\) and \(V_j\), every vertex \(v\in V_i\) has an incoming edge from some vertex in \(V_j\), and every vertex \(w\in V_j\) has an incoming edge from some vertex in \(V_i\). Equivalently, for all distinct \(i\neq j\) and every \(v\in V_i\), there exists \(u\in V_j\) with \((u,v)\in E\).

3) (No rainbow cycle) There is no directed cycle \(C\) in \(G\) that intersects each part at most once, i.e., \(|C\cap V_i|\le 1\) for every \(i\in\{1,\dots,k\}\).

Define the rainbow cycle number \(R(d)\) to be the largest integer \(k\) for which there exists a directed \(k\)-partite graph satisfying properties 1)–3) above.
problem_definition_latex: Determine asymptotically tight upper bounds on \(R(d)\) as a function of \(d\). In particular, decide whether \(R(d)=O(d)\) holds (equivalently, whether there exists a constant \(C\) such that \(R(d)\le C d\) for all \(d\ge 1\)).
latex:
Fix an integer \(d\ge 1\). A directed \(k\)-partite graph is a digraph \(G=(V,E)\) whose vertex set is partitioned into \(k\) (nonempty) parts \(V_1,\dots,V_k\), and edges may go between any ordered pair of vertices (including across parts), with no restriction on directions.

Consider directed \(k\)-partite graphs \(G=(\bigcup_{i=1}^k V_i,E)\) satisfying the following properties:

1) (Bounded part size) \(|V_i|\le d\) for all \(i\in\{1,\dots,k\}\).

2) (Pairwise in-neighborhood condition) For any two distinct parts \(V_i\) and \(V_j\), every vertex \(v\in V_i\) has an incoming edge from some vertex in \(V_j\), and every vertex \(w\in V_j\) has an incoming edge from some vertex in \(V_i\). Equivalently, for all distinct \(i\neq j\) and every \(v\in V_i\), there exists \(u\in V_j\) with \((u,v)\in E\).

3) (No rainbow cycle) There is no directed cycle \(C\) in \(G\) that intersects each part at most once, i.e., \(|C\cap V_i|\le 1\) for every \(i\in\{1,\dots,k\}\).

Define the rainbow cycle number \(R(d)\) to be the largest integer \(k\) for which there exists a directed \(k\)-partite graph satisfying properties 1)–3) above.

Determine asymptotically tight upper bounds on \(R(d)\) as a function of \(d\). In particular, decide whether \(R(d)=O(d)\) holds (equivalently, whether there exists a constant \(C\) such that \(R(d)\le C d\) for all \(d\ge 1\)).

open_problem_quote: We suspect that \(R(d)\in O(d)\). We believe that finding better upper bounds on \(R(d)\) is a natural combinatorial question, and better upper bounds to \(R(d)\) imply the existence of better relaxations of EFX allocations. Therefore, investigating better upper bounds on the rainbow cycle number is of interest in its own right, and we leave this as an interesting problem.
keywords: envy freeness up to any good
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The object is a finite digraph with bounded part size \(d\) and a simple-to-check forbidden-structure constraint (no directed cycle using at most one vertex per part) plus a local in-neighborhood condition. For small \(d\), one can brute-force or use SAT/ILP/CP-SAT to search for constructions with many parts, providing improved lower bounds or ruling out large \(k\) by infeasibility.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate solutions are explicit digraph constructions parameterized by \(d\) and \(k\); fitness can be computed automatically by checking the in-neighborhood condition and detecting a forbidden rainbow cycle (or returning a violating cycle). An evolutionary loop can explore families of constructions (e.g., algebraic, Cayley/shift-based, blow-ups) to push lower bounds on \(R(d)\) and to generate structural hypotheses that could guide proofs toward \(O(d)\) or sharper bounds.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
The rainbow cycle number \(R(d)\), introduced by Chaudhury–Garg–Mehlhorn–Mehta–Misra, asks for the largest number of parts \(k\) in a directed \(k\)-partite graph with part sizes \(\le d\) such that (i) for every two distinct parts \(V_i,V_j\), each vertex in \(V_i\) has an in-neighbor in \(V_j\) (and vice versa), yet (ii) there is no directed cycle that uses at most one vertex from each part (a “rainbow” directed cycle). The source paper proves the first general polynomial upper bound \(R(d)\le d^4+d\) via a two-stage construction: first building a cycle that may revisit one special part \(\widetilde V_\ell\), then “bypassing” repeated visits using carefully chosen intermediate parts to obtain a simple rainbow cycle. It also gives a linear lower bound \(R(d)\ge d\), leaving a wide gap and explicitly conjecturing \(R(d)=O(d)\).

Subsequent work cited in the source paper improves the upper bound by refining the bypass construction. In particular, “EFX: A simpler approach and an (almost) optimal guarantee via rainbow cycle number” exploits the observation (also noted in the MOR acknowledgments) that it suffices to prepare bypass parts only for consecutive pairs \((V_i,V_{i+1})\) along the eventual cycle, leading to an improved bound \(R(d)=O(d^3)\). Related cycle/fixed-point techniques in “Fixed-point cycles and approximate EFX allocations” are also referenced as contributing to improved rainbow-cycle-number-driven guarantees. However, none of the available citing works (including the fair-division paper “Almost Envy-Free Allocation,” which only cites \(R(d)\) as background) resolves the main asymptotic question: whether \(R(d)\) is linear in \(d\), or more generally what the tight growth rate is.

At present, the best documented general bounds remain polynomially separated, with \(d\le R(d)\le O(d^3)\) (improving the original \(O(d^4)\) bound) and the central conjecture \(R(d)=O(d)\) still open. Promising directions include sharpening the bypass/representative-set method to reduce the number of auxiliary parts needed, and exploring extremal digraph tools tailored to the strong pairwise in-neighborhood condition (a dense, multipartite analogue of minimum in-degree conditions) to force a transversal directed cycle.

sources.title: Almost Envy-Free Allocation
sources.theorem_or_result:
The paper proves several EFX/approximate-EFX existence results via weighted envy graphs, rank, and virtual value. In particular:
- For (∞,1)-bounded valuations: existence of an EFX allocation discarding at most \(\lfloor n/2\rfloor-1\) goods (Theorem 5.6) and a complete EF2X allocation (Theorem 5.7).
- For restricted additive valuations: existence of a \(\sqrt{2}/2\)-EFX allocation (Theorem 6.1).
- For (∞,1)-bounded subadditive valuations: existence of a \(\sqrt{2}/2\)-EFX allocation (Theorem 7.1).
- For restricted additive and (2,∞)-bounded valuations: existence of a complete EFX allocation (Theorem 8.1).
sources.connection:
The open problem asks for asymptotically tight bounds on the rainbow cycle number \(R(d)\) in bounded-partite digraphs (a purely graph-theoretic extremal question motivated by EFX). This citing paper is about fair-division algorithms for special valuation classes and uses envy-graph/rank/virtual-value machinery. In the provided text, it cites work on “rainbow cycle number” as motivation/background (e.g., [25], [2], [37]) but does not analyze \(R(d)\) itself, nor prove new upper/lower bounds for \(R(d)\) as a function of \(d\). Thus it does not resolve whether \(R(d)=O(d)\) or provide asymptotically tight bounds.

At most, the paper’s techniques (rankpaths, virtual values, LP feasibility preventing multiplicative cycles) are conceptually adjacent to the cycle-avoidance phenomena that rainbow-cycle-number arguments exploit, so it could be viewed as providing related tools/insight for EFX proofs, not progress on the extremal digraph parameter \(R(d)\).
